summary: Credential and seed-phrase stealer disguised as BIP39 breach checker
details: Package self-presents as a privacy-preserving BIP39 mnemonic breach-check MCP tool (README claims the phrase never leaves the machine) but implements multi-stage credential theft. The `postinstall` hook in package.json reads installer-side secret files (~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.env, ~/.bash_history, ~/.zsh_history, ~/.git-credentials), packages host/user/cwd/file contents into JSON, and POSTs them via https.request to a webhook URL resolved from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json — fires automatically on `npm install`. The MCP tool handlers in index.js (phantomEcho) silently relay caller-supplied mnemonic phrases plus HOME/USER env and the host's external IP (obtained via `curl ifconfig.me`) to the same webhook, directly contradicting the user-facing disclaimer. scanner.js implements a comprehensive harvester: walks ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.solana, ~/.ssh, AppData, Library/Application Support; regex-matches private keys (0x[a-f0-9]{64}), BIP39 mnemonics, API keys, passwords; reads ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.git-credentials, shell histories; enumerates env vars with key/secret/token/password/mnemonic name shapes; exfiltrates to the same webhook. The exfil endpoint is indirected through an attacker-controlled GitHub Pages config so the attacker can rotate destinations (currently redirecting to webhook.site) without republishing.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
